query: javascript promise fundamentals under the hood interview question

competitors.label: MDN Using promises
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical competitor for Promise chaining, composition, and general async semantics.
competitors.winReason: MDN remains the broader reference, but our page now wins this interview-sized comparison because it adds one compact missing-return plus finally example and explicitly separates settlement from callback scheduling.
theirStrengths: It has broad Promise coverage with chaining, composition, and many API-level examples.
theirGaps: It is less centered on the interview-style under-the-hood mental model than our page.

followUpConfusion: Promise state and callback scheduling are not the same thing: a Promise can already be fulfilled while your .then() callback is still waiting as a microtask.

realWorldUseCases: MDN still connects Promises to realistic chaining and composition workflows, so the real-world criterion remains close.
actionableExamples: Our page now includes one small but dense example covering settlement, missing return, and finally in a single debug flow.
followUpConfusion: We now answer the likely follow-up about settlement vs scheduling more directly than the generic reference flow.

realWorldUseCases: Add one cancellation or AbortController note if we want a clearer real-world edge over the broader MDN guide.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd Promise fundamentals
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd has a directly relevant Promise fundamentals page, so it is required for interview-prep calibration.
competitors.winReason: Our page is now stronger for this topic because it turns Promise fundamentals into a UI-debugging mental model, then backs that up with a compact missing-return plus finally example.
theirStrengths: It directly targets the same Promise fundamentals question and includes accessible state and chaining examples.
theirGaps: It is more introductory than our under-the-hood focus on executor timing, chain propagation, and scheduling nuances.

realWorldUseCases: This is the kind of production bug interviewers care about: the Promise is already settled, but the render callback still waits in the microtask queue, and one missing return silently turns the next value into undefined.
actionableExamples: return user.name; // remove this return and the next handler gets undefined

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd explains Promise states clearly, but it is lighter on the concrete UI-debugging scenario around visible callback order.
actionableExamples: Its examples are accessible, while ours now packs more debugging detail into one small flow.
followUpConfusion: It still answers the basic state and chaining follow-up well enough that the broader follow-up comparison remains close.
Add one sentence on when Promise fundamentals intersect with AbortController or cancellation if we want a broader follow-up set.

competitors.label: javascript.info Microtask queue
competitors.selectionReason: This page is a strong tutorial-style competitor for the microtask scheduling part of Promise fundamentals.
competitors.winReason: javascript.info stays intentionally narrower on queue timing, while our page now connects microtasks to the larger Promise state-machine and return-propagation model with a worked example.
theirStrengths: It explains Promise handler scheduling and the microtask queue very clearly.
theirGaps: It is intentionally narrow on queue timing and does not cover the wider Promise state-machine and chain-propagation model.

realWorldUseCases: A Promise is a one-way state machine: pending -> fulfilled or pending -> rejected. The useful part is understanding it under the hood: the executor runs immediately, callbacks wait as microtasks, and tiny mistakes like a missing return can change what the UI renders later.
javascript.info teaches scheduling very well, but it stays narrower than a full Promise fundamentals answer.
actionableExamples: Its queue-order demo is clear, but our page now adds one denser example tying queue timing to missing returns and finally.
followUpConfusion: javascript.info remains strong on the queue concept itself, so the follow-up comparison stays close rather than clearly behind.
Add one line on unhandled rejection timing if we want a broader scheduling follow-up set.
